speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Otto Schupfer took place at his home in Juliaetta on June 1st, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you start?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I started in Juliaetta in 1921. Run a little place down here, but you couldn't make it go. And then it was finally combined to do and started in Kendrick over. About 20, we started, well not 21, we got the, wait a minute, I'm wrong on that. We started on 21, but then we finally, no we didn't start that until 29. Just how, where the space went between there, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of equipment did you have in 21?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Powers, same as all of them. Troy had the same thing and it was all silent then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it wasn't a going proposition in Juliaetta?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh no, it never was a going proposition by itself anyway. Down here we took in $25 and by trying to get the musicians about $5 and about $15 for the picture and you see about how it goes. I just run it for more for the fun of it. Anything else, and Powers, I guess, Kendrick is about the same thing. It was never a money making proposition, but I figured out for one year we made 25 cents an hour, the four of us working there. That's working there and Herman, he ordered the pictures and that's the other end of it. But still it helped out, keep it make 11. And it was work that wasn't doing other times, it was night work you might say. And then these people wasn't afraid to put in a little extra time, but now you'd have to have time and a half for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did you show a movie, would it be every night or just on the weekend?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Putting every Friday and Saturday until the 4th of July or some celebration where we put in some extra. There for a little while we were on 4 a week, but that didn't work out either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now about when did you think that the Kendrick Theatre started?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think in 1929 or 1930. It started in 1918 by somebody else. Big Fish and Rene Dreff were about to write the newspaper.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It started written by Oldfield.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oldfield was the first?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, because there was a theater there before. This Oldfield bought the jewelry store in Kendrick and he had the theater equipment, moved it down from Pullman. And he wrote it for a little while, but he tried to make a living without doing anything else, but that didn't work. Then Big Fish, the printer, he run it. He did about the same things. He made another 50 cents that evening, another 50 cents more. He was working in the Gazette office during daytime, that was his job, to show there was something extra, him and his wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you charging admission?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: We charged all from 2 bits and 10 up to 75 cents I guess, about the most. Most of the time it was about 50 cents or 25 cents. Fourth of July we used to have a house full. Never charged over 25 cents then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this orchestra? You had to hire musicians?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There were some musicians down here pretty good. Barbara's wife was a pianist and another fella, he played the saxophone and they generally furnished the music. The way we run it there for a long while, we took in $20, we give them about half of it and we kept the other half.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: If they didn't make nothing, they got nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people would show up to see you in a movie on a weekend?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Sometimes 30 or 40, plus the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the entertainment for the town? Would you say that was the main entertainment for the town at the time or was there other things that were more popular?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, there was nothing bad, there wasn't even a big part of them.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There was a show down here before, I bought it from an old fella who came in here and tried to make it go and he made a failure of it. This fella, Richardson, down here, he bought the thing and he couldn't run it himself and he had to hire some so that didn't work at all. I think I bought the whole thing, the piano and the seats and one projector. Then we kept running every Friday and Saturday for a while until it finally got a little bit tougher all the time. My brother said, let's get them the highest priced shows and see what we can send our help to on them. So we got four of them, three of them the highest priced we could get, we had to pay $25 apiece plus transportation. Among them was Charlie's aunt, you probably heard of her, and up in Mabel's room and the Volga Boltman. And on those they come out, I think they're made on the forum, probably $10 or two of them, but those are the last ones to run. Then we finally got a hold of the Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Volga Boltman, was that the name of it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, it was a Russian picture. Volga River, Russia. They were darn good shows, three of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they didn't make much more money than the others did or did they?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh yeah, they did a little bit better than the others.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why did you go to Kendrick? Did you think there would be a better market up there or what?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, we had a telephone outfit that we owned. It finally got to, I think they loaned the fellas that owned the building, a brother lost some money on it and we got it. And then that's where we got the blame thing. Well, it was a stock company and they quit. McPherson couldn't make it anymore, he's paying I think $20 a month rent and he couldn't make it go anymore until he quit. The fella named Mazzell bought it. Yeah, he bought it, but he, on the first year of work, he's supposed to keep fixing up the building. Well, he tore down the building more than anything else and it ran a year for nothing. Well, then we finally started in with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you let the Juliaetta one go then? Did you stop?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, we moved the part of McPherson up there. We had two machines in then after that. Then we could run a continuous show before he always had to stop.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There used to be a fella named Hickman Bottom used to run one down here before I did. And I think he charged 10 and 25 cents and he and the light company couldn't get along so he couldn't use electricity. So he had to use carbide lights and that got way poor lights, but he had a few customers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it in the same building as yours?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, the beginning of it was, but he finally got in there and they kept moving around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did business pick up when you went to Kendrick? Did what did you say? Did business pick up?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, about the way it was going to Kendrick before. See, it wasn't shut down. The Zell had it. He put in the first talking picture because he did pretty good there for a while. But then when he quit, we just kept on going after he quit. We made a little money on it all the time, but if you make two bits an hour, there's four of us, there's a dollar an hour. And about five or six hours a day, you're in a bed and just doing nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the audiences get bigger once the talkies came in?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They did. They did. We didn't have it when they came in. The Zell had it. They had a good crowd for the first two or three shows. But then after that, it kind of got back to where it was again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it made any difference in the movies? Were they better when they started having talkies or do you think they went downhill?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, the picture was about the same.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Because it was a little bit easier to understand what you're doing about them than it was before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have to pay the orchestra though once they had sound, did you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, we didn't have to do that anymore. But we were running quite a while in Kendrick without the sound. He had to sell. That was just how that went. After he quit, we had sound from then on. The sound came in about 1927. We didn't have them to pay. We used to get the pictures from Spokane. It cost about five or six dollars. It would cost that much for both ways. But now it's cost about thirty. And you used to send them by partial post and then they kind of come up to the high end of the railroad to compete with them. They'd bring them one way you had to pay for and the other way half price. Well that worked a little bit. They kind of had to cancel that. And then Elliott Trena went to partial post again. That went up like everything and then finally come to, now it's Greyhound. That cost about twenty -five dollars each way.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, I've heard that local girls would want to become movie actresses after they'd see those films.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know whether that ended that here or not. Yeah, we had a few pretty good, this old fellow named Fred Thompson and Western. He may have played them good. Of course, that's when we had the silent down here. But then when you got the kindering, you got the talking old Gene Autry and Roy Rogers, really are a wonderful person. Shirley Temple was a plain good one.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The best money we took in the picture was, what was the name of it? Josephine, what was the name of that picture we took in the most money, Kendrick? That we took? What picture we took in the biggest crowd one time? Oh, it was a lonesome pine, wasn't it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, it was a lonesome pine.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What was that? What was that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: We took in over $300.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That was an awful lot of money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over $300 in admission?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One night?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think we were only three nights. Well, we run until one o 'clock. See, the theater was small. We always wanted the people to see the picture if it was our fault that they couldn't get in. So we run until one o 'clock.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: We run some of it three times. Was this what you took in in one night, Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Schupfer? $300?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That was the three nights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many could you fit in the movie theater?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There were 240 some seats. Oh, that's okay.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: She says 240 seats. They kept the right and the seat. We took the front ones out. Then they kept throwing stuff at the screen. They made it throw it where the bed was. They kept taking the front ones out all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the idea of throwing stuff at the screen?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They weren't as bad as here. They got in there with air rifles. The Indians were fighting over there. They shoot at them with air rifles, punch holes in the screen. We've got two of the holes. We got us all the way out the screen for a little while. About a $250 screen. It wasn't very long until we were holding it to the innocent. Then they got another big nuisance. They come in with these water pistols. That's right. A lot of people wouldn't come to show anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they squirting people in the audience?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, yeah. Just keep it squirting all over. They do that everywhere as far as that goes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you stop showing the movies in Kendrick?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: About a night after I had to stroke. I used to run the machine until Jerry Brown did. The last show was 1972, I think.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Walt Disney was the last picture we were on. Mary Poppins.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you showing it weekly until then?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Once in a while we'd run. Whenever we got a good picture come up, we thought maybe a good time we'd run it. Mary Poppins was the last show we'd run. Then we had this fellow, Jerry Brown. He liked the monkey with stuff. He kept the footjacket. We showed him that. I'd run it on Friday and he'd run it on Saturday. He didn't think I was running good enough. He'd run it on both nights. I think we'd change the paydium. Oh, about two dollars maybe, four or five something, ten hundred, try to run. Finally he'd run it most of the time. He was the same guy then. He kept on the telephone outfit. He was a good old mechanic. He wanted to go along with the telephone outfit. He got to learn the blame business real good. He owned the switchboard stuff. He was there putting stuff in, ready to get the blueprints out and study them. When they put the dial system in, he came in about an hour ago with the two main guys. Well, they did all the work, put it in. Didn't have no extra letter. He sold us equipment. We should have an installer. We got to them. They did a blame good job. This Brown, when finally he made, he never murdered very much because he couldn't pay two guys, but he'd go along in the way and see what he could learn. When Troy died at a telephone outfit, he took over. Now he got a blame good job up there by him not always asking when to get a raise in pay. He never asked about that at all. But when it worked for him, we always give him more. Now he's got a good job up there, the general manager of Troy.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But he was quite a genius. He wanted this public address system in Kendrick to get the locusts. That's one of his. He was part of the theater stuff. The first thing he did was build a public amplifier, and he took a blueprint from the theater amplifier and made an amplifier. It was just a public address system, I'd say, and play the phonograph. Excuse me, I hate to interrupt, but now I'm on the Giuliavela line and I'm getting Bud's recording. I tried, oh I see, maybe I can't. You got the wrong number. I didn't get the one on, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think? You think it's, for a guy like that, it's just natural talent that he has? Is he born with it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, natural talent. He wasn't a fella who always had how much money to get. He looked into it when they got the first vacation. He was the kind of fella that at that time, because he went along, he got nothing. As long as I had real something we needed him, we paid him better. Well, of course, that isn't the union way of doing it. You suppose they pay him big wages all the time and then when you don't need them, lay them off. Lay them off and stuff like that. We never did that. We never had them hired. But he was a plain good guy for that. And I'll call Lady and if he's ready, he can write up with me.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, I was going to ask you about, like, on the telephone and on the electric lines, some of that repair work that you'd have to do, the line work, like in the bad weather in the winter, that must have been pretty rugged.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, it was sometimes. There was one Easter Sunday, I was up on American Ridge going to have Easter dinner and my girl had to come right down to the beach and there's 23 poles down there in Kendrick.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Excuse me again.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Shall I ask A.D. if he's ready to go with me or don't I have time?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I told Arlene I'd be there by 10 .30.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Where you going, out of Moscow?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, and right over with Sam. I don't know what your plans are. I have a notion called Edith.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's recorded too, is it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It's Frank Brockey's brother -in -law that's being married today. And Margaret, his wife, my son.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were 26 poles down?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, that is in 1919, I think. The Snow Cows, they got that big around. We had 35 wires in Kendrick. Of course, that got too heavy for us. I've got a picture of it somewhere, maybe shorter than the other day. One of them turned clear over, stopped for a while. But we had them all working by night again. We got some of these local guys around here and we always had some pretty good neighbors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, was it snow?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, wet snow hangs on. Whenever it rains, then it starts to snow and that hangs on. And the wires keep on growing. There was another time we had a lot of trouble about the same. Kendrick, about four or five poles went down. That was in 1937, I guess. And at the same time, the R .E .A. was just building their line. They couldn't keep the lights on at all for a while. The wires would build up great big and then the snow would melt over the bottom one and up and flop and get the upper one out through the lights. But they've never had one quite as bad as that since that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, when you say all these poles went down, they didn't fall down. Yeah, downright they fell down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They fell down? Well, they couldn't have been in the ground too good, were they?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Not at all.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then when there's a straight line, there was a good one in the other side of the road. They take that and run with it. What really happened, an anchor broke off. Then they let the whole thing go. Yeah, they all fell down. Well, that was about here, you remember, using the country between Poma and Colfax. Same thing happened up there. There was no telephone service around just in the outer Lewis for four or five days. It happens everyplace. That's when they holler about the buried cable. You won't have that trouble, but you have other troubles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about troubleshooting the line and figuring out where the trouble is?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Let's say it's in the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use snowshoes to get around?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, not too much. I had to go up on 6th Street one Tuesday time. I wasn't very much good with them things. That's hard to walk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have them on?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they're funny to walk in. I never learned how, really. Yeah, they're funny to write. I walked about a mile up there one time. That was tough.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Okay, I'm sorry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you going to Moscow? Is that correct?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. No, I imagine he's gone. Oh, he's gone. Okay. They don't say that he came down in the desert, but he'd run down to the bank of Spokane. He left at 12 at noon, I think, and got down to about 6th and they stayed overnight here, and the next morning went back, and the same morning another train came down again. They always had the two trains every day, one each way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, Juliaetta, did it stop here for the night and then turn?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, they stopped there. They always went up to Kendrick because they had a water tank up there and a little bit different, better accommodation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they turn the train around here?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they backed down, but they had a turntable in Kendrick and they just turned around the engine. They just looked around the other end. They had a turntable in Kendrick, I suppose you've ever seen any. No. They put the engine on a rig and then a handle on each end and they turned it around and just balanced it on there. But they had another way, the Donut Arrow was the first one. It was a Y. They'd go out on one track and back up, go the other direction and that way they turned the whole thing around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it backed down into Juliaetta from Kendrick?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they often backed down to Juliaetta. They're doing the same thing now with a freight.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I'm surprised they didn't just put the turntable down in Juliaetta so it could come... Oh, there was kind of a, I don't know, politics went on. Juliaetta didn't give them the right kind of right away and Kendrick didn't, so they kind of made Kendrick the end. And then Kendrick was right ahead to that great Troy, great. That was a bad grade for them days anyway.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I'd heard something about one time there was a real bad accident coming down to Kendrick and they... Yeah, there was a bunch that left Troy.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They had two engines on and a bunch of railroad rails. I don't know where they was going with them and they couldn't hold them coming down to Kendrick. And the brakemen on the back end, they uncoupled the caboose and they stopped that. Then another fella tried to uncouple the rails and they got all down there, flopped over in the creek and killed him. Then we got down to Kendrick, the engines derailed and killed four of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the brakemen in the caboose at fault?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, there always are. There's always someone in the caboose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what I mean is, should he have cut the caboose loose? Did that make it worse?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, cut the caboose loose. That's the only thing that didn't wreck, as far as I can remember.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The rest of it was all wrecked. And there was another time there was a bunch of hogs that were in the car. They had seven car loads of hogs going on. I can just tell you remember, they were pulling up above Kendrick, they broke loose and they came down here backwards. Now, Herman and I were downtown, we seen the darn thing going by pretty good. It wasn't very far behind as an engine. And I guess they had a freight train out on a track somewhere between here and Arrow, probably on purpose, and they ran into that. They smashed all both cabooses, but they never heard a pig.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean it came clear down through Juliaetta, all the way down to Arrow? Did it what? It went all the way down to Arrow before they hit?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh yeah. Well, they had printed the same thing that happened the other day. They was loading the car up there, Kendrick, and was shaking a little bit, and finally the darn thing took off all by itself and started on the trackers on the siding. And they got down there on the D -rail, probably showed you one of those before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a D -rail?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, a D -rail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what that is, don't you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: When a car comes down the trackers, it runs it off of the track. Well, they had the car sitting there about four or five days before they got it back on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why is it that this is such a rough area for trains? Would you say it's harder than most of it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The company always wanted to ride away for nothing, and people weren't quite willing to do it that way. It seemed like Juliaetta would then give it to them, and that's how it was, and Kendrick did. Kendrick is named after a railroad engineer, so they kind of favored Kendrick, and Kendrick was a little bit ahead. They had about four or five bridges coming in there for freight. Well, Juliaetta didn't have that much, so that's a little strange, but when they got to Juliaetta with the road, it seemed like there were two stories there. The Indians' reservation, they couldn't cross it down here until they got it from it. That was one of them, and another one, I think, they run out of money as they stopped in Juliaetta for four years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Juliaetta had mostly, it was Potlatch Ridge and Fix Ridge, and was there any other ridges that Juliaetta had?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, this American Ridge up here, but that didn't go back very far. That went to Kendrick. Kendrick had Potlatch Ridge and Leland and South Flick and Bear Ridge and Texas Ridge, and Little Bear Ridge and American Ridge. They had quite a little more there. I'd have to get to Kendrick then. I think you can find that somewhere in the history books, just far as I don't know where.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Kendrick a bigger town than Juliaetta?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Neither one of them was anything. Just a street up and down town. I think I showed you a picture of Juliaetta eight years ago.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know if it did or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've seen, oh wait a minute, is that that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It was just a, what they call them, big rain shaft come along. Cloudburst. They took the railroad out down below here too. I've got some of those pictures here for that too. Here's some more of that. It rode up there from Kendrick up to American Ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's probably where you come down, wasn't it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It generally hit Kendrick worse than Juliaetta. Oh, about the same as that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It hit up in here and washed down the railroad track. I've got some pictures down here somewhere too. The railroad track down here was washed out until under the gald on Rockville.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me when I was here before that time that Haim Gardner came down and that happened, but was there, are there other times that that happened?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That was longer ago.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That is really the snow backing them out coming down across the railroad track out. But this one here was just a cloudburst up there on the hill. It took the railroad track out down here and this car rode up to Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It took out the road between here and Kendrick?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, the railroad, putting all of it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That went up far. I took up there. There was a reservation, Caldwell reservation I think opened up. Now on that same page on the bottom is another one that's marked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there a mark around one?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Birth of a nation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, did you ever see it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long ago?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It's a classic movie, so they showed it again. When I was in college they showed it there. That was by Griffith.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, that was quite a show.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That is really the beginning of it. It just showed in Lewiston. They had a big orchestra with us. Because after later it showed everywhere. It showed in Kendrick and finally too.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But you weren't going up for the reservation. You were just going to drive them. I took them up, drove up there and I think I got a lot of driving up. Then I rescued two. I didn't get nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that your Maxwell that you drove up?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, that was Overland. Blundered Jim Emmett there, Kendrick. Well, I thought that you got a Maxwell. Yeah, I did. But I was working for this Kendrick garage. He was selling Overland. I drove up there for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me a little of what it was like working in a garage back then? I mean, that was when cars were first in the country, right?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, the fella I worked for, Lewis, he drove the first car in Kendrick. To Gerald Ingalls' father. And I had to stay up there for a day showing him how to drive it. Model T Ford. And before that there was only 12 cars in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say the country, what do you mean?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, the Juliaetta, Kendrick, American Race and all the races. That was the first car I think that was sold up on the bear race. But American Race had two or three cars. But otherwise there was nothing wrong with any car. You wanted to go and win. Well, you had that for years. You could go to wintertime and win a foot or a buggy. Summertime you were the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were the cars very reliable back then or did it take a lot of work?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, if you went from here to Lewiston without a flat tire, why, you bragged about it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: At the Model T Ford, if you didn't know how to drive it, you come down the hill one time and didn't know you.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It got hotter in there, Dickens. You had to know how to drive a Model T after you got along with it all right. Put in low gear when you got too steep.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then going downhill the brakes got hot and going uphill the engine got hot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were people scared of them?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I remember one decoration day. That was 1915. It took a bunch of women up to Kendrick Cemetery and I wasn't very much a driver myself either. That was 1914 I guess it was. But before it started it backed up a little bit. I did one that let out a big holler. And of course the boss's wife along with her always says, you always got to do that before you get started. I said, you sure tell them a good lie. Well, she said they believed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard for them to learn to drive to start with?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, heck yeah. You had to spend the days with them.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The worst thing was going uphill and getting started. She wanted to get the foot off the brake because it went backwards. And then the time to step down the gas where they killed Ains. That was always the worst part of it. These automatics for anybody could drive but them things wasn't as easy as that. Then you had to shift gears. You go up and start in low and then push the clutch down, push over in the second. That was quite a job. Well, the stick shift the same way yet but that's all you had. Only the Model T Ford, the pedal down was low and that up was high. That's all there was. It was worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much money in the business of selling and repairing cars?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, of course I was working for this fellow. He had a jewelry store before. And I worked for him. I got a dollar and a half a day. A dollar and a half a day on board. The holler brought the price of gasoline to a dollar and a half a day. The gasoline was about 40 cents. That got me about four gallons of gasoline. In a day's work I get four gallons of gasoline and I go to Lewiston but I couldn't get back.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now if I got you going to how about the high price of gas?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: A lot of them get twenty five dollars a day.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: How much gasoline do you buy for that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They got no business of hollering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what could you, what was the main work that you did on the cars? Is that what you were doing? Were you like relining the brakes?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, that was on the Ford especially.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then the rest of them, there was always something to do. Park plugs, red haywire, the transmission didn't work. I was working just regular repair because I was the only one around the country. And this Lewis, the boss, he was good himself. The lady that, his wife worked there too, she was quite an energetic person. She just died here last year in Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she do? Did she work on the cars too?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, she was a bookkeeper and she got to do a little driving, did a little taxi work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It wasn't really a dray line though was it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It was just a garage. Oh it was a kindred garage.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It finally ended up by my brother and Ed Deobald got it in 1919 and then finally Ed Deobald still got it. The same garage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your Maxwell? Was that a very good car?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: For them days it was. Going uphill it always slipped out of second gear. You had a lot of that. Had an engineer come out from the factory one time, he was going to fix that. Got him worked two or three days up there on all this. I think I ordered six of them out of my freight, come in by freight. Every one of them slipped out of second gear. And he overhauled it but in about a week it just all slipped out of second gear again.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But Ed Deobald, later on in Hermann, all the cars did that. We had to figure out how to fix it. One of those engineers around me told me, oh that'll never work. We had pecks, but on second gear we'd grind a little notch in there so they'd stick there. Oh that you couldn't shift gears, all kinds of other things. Ed Deobald after that he fixed a lot of them. Putting all the cars in second gear, it always slipped out of second going uphill. And Ed Deobald fixed a lot of them up there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That was one big job for a long while then. And then sold gasoline. The gasoline service was better then than now. You'd always get gasoline on Sunday and the holidays.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know I was wondering about them cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people want to get them right away or did it take years before a lot of people trusted cars instead of horses? You mean for buying them?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah for buying them. Oh they definitely got them right away. The first year worked up there we didn't have a car. The second year we had a car, Lewis had a car load shipped in like so. And he sold them there in no time. And then it wasn't very long after that he changed over to sell overland. He sold a lot of them, but quite a few now for the rest of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the townspeople the first to get them or was it the farmers?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh about the same.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There wasn't so very much then that you could only pay a dollar down a dollar next week. You didn't have to pay for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right when you bought them? The whole thing?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. The maxes were $850. Most of them paid for them. I guess the boss knocked off a little bit for cash.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Alexander's store? Did I know him? Did you, Nana?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh yeah. I know that Apon Adams that died up there last year.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Did you know him?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I knew who he was. He worked for Alexander's store and he married with his Alexander's sister. And he's the bookkeeper down there for years at Alexander's store. And then when the store burned up, my brother was working for an electric light company, Wilmont's. Herman and Wilmont's both wanted to go to California and they didn't have no bookkeeper. I actually could take an electric in on the telly and by the way, Herman got his, he said, Get this Apon Adams up there. And he did. That's how he got started at Washington Water Power. He worked for Wilmont and then that spring, he went to just go into a winter. Him and I read the meters and did all the line work. We hired them outside of Watson and Whiteford, you know, the Kenting and Troy. And then Adams stayed with the Washington Water Power until he got those to retire. Well, Herman did too. Because he didn't, he stayed with Wilmont and then went on for the water power.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay with the electric line business too?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I never stayed with it but I could if I wanted to. I got into the telephone business before instead of that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Is Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alexander's sister still alive?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, she died here about six or seven years ago. She taught school in Kendrick, Juliaetta and then in Kendrick. When she lived in Moscow, she drove to Genesee and taught school there for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Alexander like, the man?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, pretty good sort of a guy. We all kind of liked him. Good, darn good. Merchants as far as I go. He had a good story here. I understand he was Jewish. Yeah, I think he was a Jew and then he had a brother -in -law in Kendrick Beckwith run the store up there. But I don't know whether he was a Jew or not. He started selling cars just before Lewis did. He sold the big stuff, the Hudson's. I think he was mostly Hudson's and Detroit. Some of them, Detroit I think they call it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Alexander had a good story. He had one in Lewis and that was his brother I guess or uncle or something.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I heard that Durnham and Kaufman had a store in Kendrick in the early days and they had one in Moscow.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Durnham and Kaufman.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Were they related, Alexander? Or do you know?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know but I think they were Jewish too.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, well you know what Jews invented? What?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Interest.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They invented interest. They tell them they made a living anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think Alexander tried to take advantage of people?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I don't know. I don't think so.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I've heard he was pretty good. A darn good merchant you might say. I talk about the store business, but a little later that child, Seinoble's grandfather was down there under store in Juliaetta. But here then Charles' father under store for a while. Charles, he's about the fourth generation or third with the store in Juliaetta.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: When you were doing that...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I'll get over that way.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: If some of the railroad come in to be interested, I'd give them that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I'll tell you, I think...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Then he thought he gave it to the railroad, instead they thought he gave it to Vollmer and he sold it to the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your folks gave it to Vollmer?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they thought they gave it to the railroad, instead they gave it to Vollmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they thought they were giving it to the railroad?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, the railroad. He was right. I think this goes down first. He's inside. Yeah. Yeah, that's in just about Tina's best days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you mean your folks didn't know that they were giving it to Vollmer?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, until later on I guess. You've probably seen the paper where Vollmer sold them.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now turn your rig off for that. I want to tell you some more about that railroad. That deal. They would give nobody any money for a right of way. Well, they didn't think that was terrible because the Washington Water Power gave them so damn much. Well, if they hadn't, if the farmers, all of them the way that wanted it, well, they would have got the electricity for nothing. That's what most of them was after. And they're the same way with a telephone. Outfifty one out of fifty. Well, you can build across my line, but I'd have to have a free telephone. Used to be real strong that way. And those electric lights are the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did the telephone company handle that? Would they go along with that and give them a free phone?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No. You pay for the right of way and you pay for the telephone. But the worst one come in when the light line is built to Kendrick, they come down over the American range above where the road is. There's an old road up there. And what they call him, a fellow named Helpman up there, he gave them the help, he gave them the right. This dunker, he didn't know where. He's the fellow I went to Spokane with. He didn't know much about it because he said he could have the right of way to give me the free electricity. Well, he fell for that. Well, when the free electricity come in, he never used it up down the hill. He said, I'll take your free electricity in town for a second. Well, they did for a while then. I was turning my free electric light bill over to my son -in -law, Frank Boyd. He was heating the house and everything else. Then Wilmont had a line and he was going to have to rebuild that darn thing, just get out on the road. Because he wanted this free electricity. You couldn't do that. Then we built a line, we took a hole down below and this Wilmont, he wasn't very good at it and he never got the right of way. Had a hole all done and the fellow down below says, this electric light company is not very reliable. He says, I'll have to have some money for that or get out on the road. We had to take a hole down the road again. So that's the way they treated you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, like you take that railroad guy coming through, what would he do if you wouldn't have given him a ride away for a hundred bucks?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They can condemn it and go through in a way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what they do?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, that's what they do. I guess they had to do that on some of them. I know that's what the highway department does. Yeah, the railroad just could do the same thing because one fella can't stop them but a lot of them tried it. Well, there's a lot of that crazy things went on. Yeah, there's free ride away business. That was a joke. Well, we had a little bit on the water business here. See, there's a spring up above here. It's got a sound hat. And they, to begin with, a dirty trick went on there. The spring is right up on the upper part of the field up here. And my cousin, he put this in strawberries down here. And back in this fella here over here, he kind of burned a spring away. He got through with it and he took it away from him. So he lost the spring and later the town wanted a spring. There's Martin Thomas up here that owned the whole workshop up there. He sold it to the town for a thousand dollars. And this fella over here that got it took it away from my cousin. They finally gave him, they crossed this place five hundred dollars. And they gave us down here twenty -five dollars across the south. They crossed this just a quarter of a mile, which is okay. The fella down here, they give him, I think, a hundred dollars across here. Well, then come on later, the fella owned this place down here. Him fell out of the water system. He said he wanted to cross, and they wanted free water. So he come up with a petition. He was assigned it. He said, we're going to have to get free water for crossing our right -of -way. Oh, that'd be a dirty trick. I said, I'm not going to sign that. And so, well, he said, I'll get it. I said, I don't think you will. And so he, uh, I wasn't paying him. That's why they just shut the water off on him. They got us, we turned it back on again. They saw the pipe off. Then once they had an old well out there, he's going to have that well fixed up. And he says he's going to get that free water. This fella named Halliday is the only one that do that. He knew what was going on. He still cost about a hundred dollars to fix that well up. He said, well, that is too much. By the time he got through with it, he paid his water bill. And he was a rich farmer in this country. He lived down here. So that's the way they got it. Well, then he's, when he finally seen that I wouldn't sign up for free water, he didn't even go up any farther to get them fellas. But he says they probably forgot that. And the funniest part of it is, when they're looking down the records down here, they, on a men's book, somebody tore out those pages with that on it. But they, well, they bought this right away. They didn't even make it work.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So, so the upshot was he didn't, he didn't get no free right away. No.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But they all wanted some free, an REA come in. They, they wasn't paying a thing for right away. Well, I can see why. But some of these guys wanted a free electricity the rest of their life for the right away across the place. They had a good system.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The REA was giving everybody a good deal.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, because the government money, but they wouldn't buy, they wouldn't give anything for right away. Well, they, they gave me kind of a dirty hand again. Now they crossed our place up here and, and we put on the down, they just, they pay for all damages. Well, they wouldn't, they put on their vendor, I got to find a feedback again, they say they changed that. They scratch that off, they were all damages. Always, we didn't want to sign them, but we're finally get them and go on while we sign this. Well, it wasn't very long when they built our line. We had our piece of land in our foul fence. They just, they didn't open the fence, they just drove through it and tore it to pieces and went down through it and fixed it all up again and right as it went down the second time to everything to pieces, because they didn't have to pay anything because that was on the, on the contractor. And when you said anything to them about it, well, you get after the contractor. Well, the contractor says that, we suppose that was a free right away going to where it is. They had a dirty deal they was pulling on you.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But some fellas looked ahead far enough, I played with the parks up here, the line, the line kind of come up here and we had to go across here another field. And because they wanted to cross the fence line here, he wanted a big price for it. Because they wouldn't pay it, so they built a line around here for about three times the price.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Because there was, there was that free right away, there's two sides to that. Some of them wanted too much free.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's where we had a telephone line over in the Clearwater and it's pretty hard to get over that horse and buggy days as a farmer over there. It's hard to get over it, I'll take care of real line this winter. And that won't charge you very much. Well, then when you got spring, he took more money than all those farmers put in over there. He was going out every day fixing telephone lines.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's a lot of tricks to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the same way I say about this, you'll get that turned on, have you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, I put it back on a little while ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That bad a story, huh?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, about this, the out there and... ...the telephone and the, oh, and the, and you know, and your brother's electric line and all that. Well, that's a community service, so you could take advantage of people, but that... ...from here to Kendrick, you couldn't get up there on the foot or with a horseback, which I never liked, or with a buggy, and we turned to get on the... ...the train left up at eight o 'clock, went to Kendrick and got on it, and later on, there was another come back at two o 'clock, didn't have much to do, come back on it. If you couldn't run, there's another come back, and if you come back at nine o 'clock, and then there's another way out of it, you could walk down the railroad track. About half the time we did that, there's a man and I overhauled the line above Kendrick, we hauled all the material up there on first of the week, then the next day we got on the train, went up to Kendrick, worked till five o 'clock and walked home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this a freight that'd be going up today?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, it was a passenger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A passenger train?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they had good passenger service here for a while. How many trains a day was going from... Two going up and two going down, but one went up in the morning, eight o 'clock, they went from eight to nine, and it was back and forth in there, sometimes changed, but that was a way hander from Kendrick, and then I took care of that one there, but Adams and I took care of electric lights out in Troy, that was real handy too, we'd go up to Troy in the morning, read the meters up there, we'd get through at noon, when we'd get back about noon again, and then we'd come back at a night train at nine o 'clock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see many hobos riding the rails?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, not too many. They used to make evolutions, they'd come around here, want something to eat, but they disappeared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have any camps along in through here where they jungled up?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, there was one in Kendrick that they kind of erected for them, and they had a boxcar they kind of lived in every time, cooked up. They cooked the beans and stuff, but the railroad finally stopped that, and down here they had to go to a hobo camp, and they'd lay up, and next day they'd be on the road again.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They mostly ride on freight trains, but the freight trains, once they're accommodated, they might have stopped any more of these towns. I used to ride along Kendrick, about the time I had it, I'd get back about four or five o 'clock when a freight train came down, and I'd get on it. They always stopped down here, but they finally don't stop down here, and it got a little worse. We had to go to, had our telephone trouble down at Arrow one time, and we walked down, that's eight miles, and there's Herman and I and the three of us, and we got down, and it was just about time we'd make that train up four o 'clock, and we couldn't let Herman, he got on the side, and he said we didn't get on until he got off too. Well, then we tried, that's what happened, we got us working for the bell system. If we wanted to hire anybody, bring us back, that was okay, so we got after the go -down, section boss done it. Well, first he'd take us up, I think, for $10. We wouldn't have to pay it, and he finally backed out. The only way to get back to Juliaetta was a foot, but then a freight train came up at nine o 'clock, so we waited for that freight train, but it went on and never stopped, and Juliaetta went to Kendrick, but then there was a passenger train down at nine o 'clock, and we got back, all right, that day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were gonna ride a freight, say, like this one from Arrow, where would you get on it? Where? What part of the train would you ride?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, we generally got as close to the end as we could because the cold burners, the dino cinders was awful bad to get back for them.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They wouldn't care if you got on a freight. Oh, some of them would kick you off, if you seen it, and others didn't care. Some of them were pretty horrible, but the rail was pretty bad, and the railroad, to them, they thought they was very important people. You couldn't have had to tip your hat to the conductor on a passenger train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of these guys on the train local, or were they mostly from elsewhere?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they finally got the local. I can't put any getting on that deal myself. There was a fellow named a freight tank helping on a telephone outfit in 1913 and 12. He couldn't have turned the railroad. He couldn't have wanted me to join with him, and I didn't, but he finally did. He got to be the engineer on the freight train. First thing, next thing, he was on the passenger, and the last job he had, run this down one car down through here. I asked him all the time. I used to ride with him up to the front end.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, why don't you retire?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was as old as I am.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Why should I retire, get $1 ,100, and I'm sipping on a stool four hours a day?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But they finally sold the stool out from under him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think these guys expect you to tip your hat to them?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, that was so much important. They were really that important job.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Some of them were pretty nice, but most of them, well, they brought them two dominated years ago from the, on the passenger train back east, it was bad. Now, we used to have them away from there back in Chicago, and our daughter's going to St. Louis, and we tried to, we had to, we had our ticket round trip, but he couldn't get the reserved seat coming back. He said, we'd have to get back there. So we went in and asked for it, and oh, we can't do a thing for you around Christmas, and you'd have to wait a little how long, and well, that kind of ended that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And so our wife, well, my daughter's father -in -law come back here too, and said, how are you making it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Always like a reserved seat right away. So we went down there and asked him again. Oh, he couldn't do anything about it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And by his own age, how'd he get it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he says, finally, finally, he said we'll get on the, we'll go to some other place, Union Pacific. He said we can get to one as far as Denver, but that's as far as you get to a reserved, then you have to take your own again. So we got a ticket to Denver, and when we got to Denver, there's a brakeman. He was a really nice sort of a guy. He said, whatever you do, don't get out of the station. They'll never want you back on, but he's, get back on again someplace. And we said, we were supposed to change trains there when we wanted to get back, conduct it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, another room for you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I says, well, okay, and they got one back. There was a fellow standing there in the door right over there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, any room in there for us to get back to up to Spokane?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I've got lots of room here, but they say up there, the regular class I've got, maybe this is first class. Well, I said, we don't give a damn to first class. What it is, we want to get to Denver. He said, well, get on. So we got on, and nobody in the car but some wife and I, and we going on, here come the conductor. He says, I told you you couldn't get on. Yeah, you told me it's clear full, you told a damn big lie, there's nobody in here. Mrs.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Meingastner?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Now the greatest saloon keeper ever was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was rare in those days, wasn't it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yes, they had a saloon down here, they had a brewery, well, they had a brewery down here the years. When we went to school, that was our whistle at noon, it was a brewery whistle. Of course, they changed hands, this fellow, he just runs a saloon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I know the one fellow up here, he says, you know the woman down there running that saloon?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, it's funny, but what does he go there now and see, the women are running saloons. They had to hold them more orderly than they are now. They wouldn't get that darn noisy and rowdy as they do now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she, when she run the saloon, do you think that some of the local people, like some of the local women, would be able to get a saloon? Would they look at that and say they didn't like it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I suppose they figured she was kind of an outcast, but she was a German woman and couldn't talk English very good, so she had not much to do with anybody anyway.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Did she ever have to, I wonder if she ever did have a hard time keeping order in there. I don't know, they might have, I don't know about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was a, was she the barkeeper herself, the bartender?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, her husband was running the, well she was running the bar and the whole thing. But it wasn't like it as now as it goes, they get so darn noisy and the more you drink, the more beer they give you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It wasn't like that then? Huh? It wasn't the same way then?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, it wasn't, they didn't get so darn noisy. They'd probably sit in and play cards with a bottle of beer and on the table. That was about the only entertainment they had then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Except to go to the movie theater. But, you know, there wasn't any of them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this back before?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That was before.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, the first movie theater I saw was here, and when folks are going to see the moving pictures, we couldn't figure out what that was. And we went down there, they had a sort of cranked up hand and awful poor lights and the guy on film, instead of winding down a reel, he went down in a clothes basket. That's where they come from, they're so darn explosive, you know, when they catch a fire, they really burn. Then he winds it back up on the other reel again. And I remember yet there was a train coming right towards you and oh, did the people holler and look like a train to run over them. We were just kids then. That's the first movie picture I saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that at, Lewiston?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, down in Juliaetta.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you go to Lewiston much when you were young?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, we got down once or twice to the fair. They generally run a train. They go down here in the morning, the railroads, back in the evening. We used to ride on those once in a while.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's just for the fair. They used to go about the same way as Spokane. At the end they put an extra car on the Spokane, have a little bigger crowd, a little discount.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would you go to Spokane? Maybe once or twice a year?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Heck no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, probably once every... I think I never got to Spokane until I was about 10, 12 years old. And then probably once or twice after that,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But Lewiston would be a couple times a year?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh no, not by train. Didn't get to Lewiston until I got the car. Then we kind of got down there maybe once or twice a year. You couldn't afford to drive down there every week because work the day took off, couldn't get enough gasoline to go down there and back. And the tires only run about 1 ,500 miles and you had to buy no tires. Then they had a lot of expenses being so high now, they were sure higher then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, I wanted to ask you about the wine business here in Juietta. I understand there was a winery here in the early days.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, if you was here the other day, I showed you, I had a picture of the people that run it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: If you want that, I'll turn that off a little bit, I'll find the... I'll give a picture to somebody too. It shows the way they scrape, the way they raise the grapes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that thing set up? Was it the same people that run the winery that raise the grapes for it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, the wine cellar is still down here. A part of it, a part of it down... I thought I remember Frank Everly. I gave a picture of him and his wife, to Nobles down there. Herman's wife had it up there, and Herman had it, and I gave it to them. Well, Herman got it in the album, but a lot of folks had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, was that a going business for them?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I guess it was. I don't know, it must have been about 20, he finally sold and left.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I suppose it did, he made quite a little bit. I used to get down to the wine cellar, see the big barrels sitting in there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you know, when they started this winery up in Troy, it made me think about this, that I heard that there had been one down here years and years ago.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, well, there was one later, the fella still down there, he had one six or seven years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Down here?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, he's still down there. He is, but he's still got a grape. Seems like it don't take very many grapes to make wine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this guy have many, this Everly have many acres of grapes?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think he had about three acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, you raised bees, didn't you, for a while? Didn't you make honey? How did you, what was the deal on that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, we just got it for our own honey, but nobody in the family writes the honey anymore, and I didn't like to be stung once in a while, so we quit.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They ended up with kind of a nuisance, anytime you wanted to go somewhere, maybe on Sunday they'd go to bees in the swarm, you had to take care of them. Of course, they do it different now, they take the queen out and put the swarm back together again, and they don't have the swarm. Maybe leave the country. And you know, they're having this big bee howl down here. We probably had it in the Russian paper. Well, ours all died here one year, and we had to empty hives up there. The next year some of them come in. What killed them we never knew. There was no spray, I assure you that. And everybody else, I guess, around there. My uncle used to be quite a bee raiser. But I think his probably all died then too, because nobody had any more, but some come in from somewhere, getting the hives themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you provide the hives for them?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use for hives then?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, made a box of the regular beehives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I got rid of my beehive last year, you know what I did with it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Gave it this color Amrit Kendrick. He couldn't raise bees.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: My uncle used to race. He sold quite a little honey. I think he sold about a dollar a gallon. But you know, a gallon of that stuff, weight 12 pounds, gosh, is heavy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people use honey a lot more than they do now, would you say?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, maybe about the same.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I was wondering, maybe they used it instead of sugar. Well, instead of syrup you could have, but they, it didn't sponge away properly. I think you'd probably buy it in the nuts now, if they're too thin. If it was too cheap, they wouldn't buy it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What about the, wasn't there a flour mill in...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Juliaetta?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: My uncle Florian down there come into this country in 1888, and the first job he had was digging the ditch. He used to work for that flour mill. He used water power.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Then they used the water power, like the town, that was 1940s, they made electricity with it. And then they finally pumped the water out of the creek up to the reservoir, and that all went, and the power comes from the same place.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Used to be a flour mill in Juliaetta, one in Kentigan, another one in Leland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The same man owned the flour mill for a long time?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he kind of stayed in the family, then went out, then got back again. The fellow Holbrook built the blame thing. He'd run it quite a while, and then he sold the blame thing to different ones and found a fellow named Vince and got it, and he run the light plant together, mostly light plants. And then he quit the flour mill business, run the light plant, and some fellow in the worse, they come down, they're gonna show him how to make money on the lights, and they muck it around there and measuring the water and measuring the electricity and put the dynamo upstairs where it used to be downstairs and gather on the wire up to the window and the thing burned up.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And they couldn't figure out where to make any electricity for nothing anyway. It got too small, the town got bigger and the rate come down. It used to be, I think, 45 cents a kilowatt. You see, now the Washington Water Bar is about a cent and a half.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Was that supplying electricity just to the businesses in town, or was it... Oh, we finally run it clear out the air.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Anybody wanted light, they had a, there was no meters on it, they had a dollar and a half for the 50 cents a light a month, I guess that's what it was. It has little candle power, it made about as much light as a 25 watt, not that much, the old carbon light. Then I used to work for them about ever so often. They'd get on the power plant, somebody had to stay there at night and all they had to do was sleep there all night. Nothing to do, I used to stay there and got about 50 cents a night out of it, or maybe nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you supposed to do while you were there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Sleep. You started in the evening, you shut off in the morning. Of course, I never got into it, but they were muskrats that dig a hole in the ditch. Of course, in the north time the hole, ditch went out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Muskrats?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they dig a hole and then the water started coming out through the hole and that made the hole big in a hurry, I guess. Of course, then they had a, there were no lights that night after that anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they just, that electricity was only for at night?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they began with it only at night and they finally, this Holbrook again, he bought it back again and I helped them then and we put meters on all of them. There was an awful lot of kicking went on, but then they had enough power then for a while. The first time they got enough power again was about 1915, they changed from the carbon lights to these master lamps. See the master lamps, it took five kilo, five watts for a candle power. The master that took one watt, so that helped them like everything. Then they kept on running again, running again, put the meters in and of course they kicked like everything but the town got bigger and they didn't have enough power and then they started running at a date time. But first they just started running at Monday for wash day and I think Wednesday for ironing day when it started to sound like the irons, there wasn't any before that. And then finally the thing went up in smoke. Then they had no light for about a couple of years.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So that sounds like a pretty good business to be able to use the same thing for making flour and providing electricity, that's a nice combination.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But a farmer run that and he wasn't on to it at all, he didn't charge one enough you might say, he didn't make a living on it, he was a retired farmer, he finally bought it and they kick on electricity too high, they let them have it cheaper and that don't work. They have more light than by the time he was running in a Juliaetta over street lights than any place in the country and I think he got $20 for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it provided street lights for the town too, eh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what kind of lights were these? Were these the carbide?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, they were carbide.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They kind of fit just on a kind of horseshoe in it. I don't know if I could show them, I don't know where to find it. But they just looked way, compared with now, they were way dim, about like a cooler lamp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say Juliaetta had more street light than most of the other towns?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, for the size of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's Vince and he's, I don't know, maybe you know Norley Callison?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Well, his Norley son, that is Bob, his wife's uncle run it, or the grandfather run it. He was the last one to run the thing. But he wasn't much of a businessman. He charged too much for it, that's okay, get a pen with the sportsman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember, did the flower have a name? That they put out here?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They got a sack down there at the Nobel store. And we could take a sack of wheat, a bushel of wheat I guess, and get a sack of flour. And they kept what's come out of there for their profit. There's bran and stuff like that, they kept that, that you get the flour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of set up was it in the mill?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What was the,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was it really run?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well it began with, I didn't see that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it water power to start with?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, it began with, they had these guys on old mill stones, great big stones, I never seen them run, I seen the stones laying around later on, but then they had the same kind of mill equipment as you see in the big mill now. Only run ball bearing. They run the thing, I think three or four times, first they run it through the one machine and took the flour out, and after another machine took it out. They had the same, called down the bleached, bleached in those kind of funny outfits. Kendrick had a bleacher outfit, they made a spark, they made a gas, the only gas. Then they run the flour through the, this gas that come from the outer to white, bleached it. And down Juliet it did the same darn thing, I think they had muriatic acid run down over a piece of iron and made the same gas. It needs to color their flour that way. But it didn't make the flour any better, but that was later put on. They had the same equipment as the big mill, only not as much of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of engine was it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Just water power.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just water power?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They run the mill at daytime, and the creek is a little bigger then. And the light plant at nighttime, it really figured out very good. Kendrick had no lights, well they did have some before that, but they didn't, it didn't improve out, they quit that. And then for a long while Kendrick didn't have anything. When they started working in the garage, Kendrick didn't have no electric lights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They used steam for a while?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Kendrick did, they began with. And then they finally, this fellow went with me to that Spokane Dunkup, he put in what they call a semi -diesel in one cylinder. And they run those for a while, but the fellow put up the money, maybe I told you this before, I don't know whether that's in Herman's history or not, the fellow put up the money, he's the first thing this fellow did, he came out of college. And it was his father -in -law gave him enough money to put in, give him $10 ,000, gonna put in a power plant in Kendrick. He looked the thing over and there was plenty of water there. He had to put in a power plant right there. He got a franchise of the lights, a pretty darn good one for the street lights. And they went on, they figured out there was no water there, so he got a couple of these semi -diesel engines and did one cylinder. And they finally got the darn thing a going and finally the Washington Water Power had Troy, they gave him Troy for nothing. So he run a line from Kendrick to Troy and run both of them for a while. And that and both of them from the old Wilmont, well if you write that book up there, Herman mentions him, he told me, we were pretty good friends, he said, you know that thing don't work. He said, hey, he's got an engineer hired and he's himself and the darn thing don't bring him much money to pay both of them. He said, I don't get nothing out of him that don't even get interest. And he finally fired the engineer and I guess the other guy too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He hired another fellow, Gene Taylor, you remember him didn't you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He hired his uncle. He run the darn thing for a while and it didn't go quite so bad, but he said, it still out goes bigger than the income. He began to build a line from Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From Kendrick?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: From Kendrick, hook under Washington Water Power. That's a bad way ever since then. He kind of paid out them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how did it happen that Herman and you got involved in this business?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I thought the summit changed. You got to turn that off again. But this is a story that we should have about how you guys got involved in this business.
